Instead, their late-game behavior remains conditional and opportunistic: they adjust contributions up when the group as a whole still looks reasonably cooperative, and they reduce them when the environment seems more mixed or deteriorating, even very close to the final round. Near the end, the group moves toward intensified reciprocal rewarding and continued high contributions, with explicit discussion of “last round” behavior. In that context, the player’s contribution behavior actually becomes more polarized: they fully contribute in one of the final late rounds but then free-ride completely in the last round. This pattern suggests an end-game effect: when cooperation is highly coordinated and the game is almost over, they feel freer to extract personal benefit by not contributing, especially if they still expect substantial redistribution from others’ contributions. At the same time, they continue to engage in reward exchanges and receive substantial rewards in the final round, so they do not fully defect from the social system; they only reduce their public-good input. They do not verbally justify this end-game free-riding. Overall, they show some classic endpoint opportunism in contributions, while remaining socially embedded in the rewarding network.

This indicates that they do not follow a purely backward-induction, payoff-maximizing logic. At the same time, late in the game they abandon costly reward usage entirely, despite having used it heavily in the mid-game. That shift happens well before the final round and then stabilizes; from that point on they play a simpler pattern of alternating between cooperative contributions and full free-riding without spending on rewards. This suggests that, as the end approaches, they streamline their strategy: they keep using contribution levels to condition on others but drop the extra cost of shaping behavior via rewards, perhaps inferring that there is not enough remaining horizon for reward investments to pay off. They do not display a late-game escalation of cooperation for its own sake, nor a full decay to defection. Instead, they end roughly as they behave mid-game: sometimes cooperative, sometimes exploitative, with the main end-game adaptation being the cessation of reward spending.
